title: Reproducible pipeline

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Reproducible pipeline; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: scikit-learn User Guide; 1.9.x target, accessed 2026-07; Getting Started plus User Guide sections 3 and 7: pipelines, reproducible estimator configuration, and evaluation; atomic topic: Reproducible pipeline
sources.claim: A reproducible AI pipeline records code, environment, data and split versions, preprocessing, configuration, seeds, fit artifacts, evaluation outputs, and commands while separating fit, evaluation, and reporting.

sources.locator: NIST AI Risk Management Framework; AI RMF 1.0; revision status monitored; AI RMF lifecycle profiles, documentation, accountability, monitoring, residual risk, and management outcomes; atomic topic: Reproducible pipeline
sources.claim: A reproducible AI pipeline records code, environment, data and split versions, preprocessing, configuration, seeds, fit artifacts, evaluation outputs, and commands while separating fit, evaluation, and reporting.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A reproducible AI pipeline records code, environment, data and split versions, preprocessing, configuration, seeds, fit artifacts, evaluation outputs, and commands while separating fit, evaluation, and reporting.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A clean environment rebuilds a pinned pipeline from a data checksum and split manifest, reproduces metrics within a declared tolerance, and stores the report with artifact hashes. Your goal is to reproduce a recorded experiment from a clean environment and explain remaining nondeterminism. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Evaluation and responsible AI; Language, perception and embodied applications; Project card and experiment plan; git tools dependencies; Train, validation, test and leakage. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: A reproducible AI pipeline records code, environment, data and split versions, preprocessing, configuration, seeds, fit artifacts, evaluation outputs, and commands while separating fit, evaluation, and reporting. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Hardware and parallel libraries can remain nondeterministic, external data changes, seeds do not capture every source, cached state can hide steps, and exact repeatability differs from independently consistent results. The learner must reject this overclaim: Setting random_state once is sufficient to reproduce any ML experiment on another machine.

includes: Pin environment and data version, seed stochastic components and separate fit, evaluation and reporting steps.
Reproduce a recorded experiment from a clean environment and explain remaining nondeterminism.
excludes: Hardware and parallel libraries can remain nondeterministic, external data changes, seeds do not capture every source, cached state can hide steps, and exact repeatability differs from independently consistent results.

outcomes: Reproduce a recorded experiment from a clean environment and explain remaining nondeterminism.

examples.title: Reproducible pipeline: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A clean environment rebuilds a pinned pipeline from a data checksum and split manifest, reproduces metrics within a declared tolerance, and stores the report with artifact hashes.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A clean environment rebuilds a pinned pipeline from a data checksum and split manifest, reproduces metrics within a declared tolerance, and stores the report with artifact hashes.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Version code, environment, data, split, configuration, and artifacts, record all controllable seeds, and report remaining platform or algorithmic nondeterminism.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Version code, environment, data, split, configuration, and artifacts, record all controllable seeds, and report remaining platform or algorithmic nondeterminism.

examples.title: Reproducible pipeline: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Two GPU runs differ slightly despite one seed, so versions, deterministic settings, and variation are recorded rather than claiming bitwise identity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Two GPU runs differ slightly despite one seed, so versions, deterministic settings, and variation are recorded rather than claiming bitwise identity.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Hardware and parallel libraries can remain nondeterministic, external data changes, seeds do not capture every source, cached state can hide steps, and exact repeatability differs from independently consistent results.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: Setting random_state once is sufficient to reproduce any ML experiment on another machine.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Reproduce a recorded experiment from a clean environment and explain remaining nondeterminism.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Setting random_state once is sufficient to reproduce any ML experiment on another machine.
misconceptions.correction: Version code, environment, data, split, configuration, and artifacts, record all controllable seeds, and report remaining platform or algorithmic nondeterminism.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: Setting random_state once is sufficient to reproduce any ML experiment on another machine.

analogy.example: a reviewable experiment package containing scope, baseline, data/version manifests, evaluation, model card, risk register, and reproduction commands can introduce Reproducible pipeline only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: project design, reproducibility, oversight documentation, and capstone defense
limitations: documentation and a polished demo are bounded evidence, not production authorization or proof of safe capability

faded.prompt: Quick check for “Reproducible pipeline: bounded direct case”: state its starting evidence, apply the Reproducible pipeline rule, and give the observable result in three short lines.
scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.
expectedEvidence: Named example: Reproducible pipeline: bounded direct case.
Starting evidence: A clean environment rebuilds a pinned pipeline from a data checksum and split manifest, reproduces metrics within a declared tolerance, and stores the report with artifact hashes.

recap.summary: You should now be able to reproduce a recorded experiment from a clean environment and explain remaining nondeterminism. Keep this boundary in view: Hardware and parallel libraries can remain nondeterministic, external data changes, seeds do not capture every source, cached state can hide steps, and exact repeatability differs from independently consistent results.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Reproducible pipeline with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.
Why is this overclaim invalid: Setting random_state once is sufficient to reproduce any ML experiment on another machine.
Apply the boundary to: Two GPU runs differ slightly despite one seed, so versions, deterministic settings, and variation are recorded rather than claiming bitwise identity.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Model card and human oversight”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
